questions.prompt: MM Proposition I (no taxes, no distress, no agency, no info asymmetry) says:
choices: Firm value V_L = V_U; capital structure is irrelevant
Firms should be 100% debt to maximize value
Firms should be 100% equity to minimize risk
Cost of equity equals cost of debt
questions.explanation: In a frictionless market, slicing the value pie differently doesn't change its size. The arbitrage proof shows V_L must equal V_U.

questions.prompt: Unlevered cost of capital r_U = 12%. Cost of debt r_D = 5%. The firm has D/E = 0.5 (no taxes). What is the cost of equity per MM II? Answer as a percent.
Under MM (no taxes), what is WACC at the cost of equity above (D/E = 0.5, r_U=12%, r_E=15.5%, r_D=5%, D/V=1/3)?
questions.explanation: WACC = (2/3)(15.5) + (1/3)(5) = 10.33 + 1.67 = 12%. Equals r_U; that's MM II's mechanical implication.

questions.prompt: Which assumptions does MM I rely on (so its violations are what drive real-world capital structure)?
choices: Risk-free borrowing for everyone at the same rate
questions.explanation: All four are required for the MM frictionless setting. Real-world deviations come from violating each of these.
